question: A 72-year-old woman is found in her apartment during a heat wave. The air conditioning is broken. She is confused and her skin is hot and dry. Temperature is estimated above 104°F. HR 128 BP 90/60. What is happening and what should you do?
option_a: Heat exhaustion. Give her water to drink
option_b: Heat stroke. This is life-threatening. Begin immediate aggressive cooling by removing clothing applying cool water and fanning. Do not give fluids by mouth as she is confused. Transport emergently
option_c: She just needs to rest in a cooler area
rationale: Hot dry skin altered mental status and high temperature indicate heat stroke where thermoregulation has failed. This is immediately life-threatening. Begin aggressive cooling immediately. Do not wait for transport to start cooling.

question: A 25-year-old construction worker feels weak dizzy and nauseated after working in 95°F heat. He is sweating heavily pale and has a headache. Temperature is 100°F. HR 100 BP 110/70. He is alert and oriented. What condition is this?
option_a: Heat stroke requiring aggressive cooling
option_b: Heat exhaustion. Move him to a cool environment remove excess clothing and give water or sports drinks since he is alert. Rest and rehydration usually resolve symptoms. Monitor for deterioration to heat stroke
rationale: Heavy sweating weakness nausea and mildly elevated temperature with intact mental status indicates heat exhaustion. The body is still attempting to cool through sweating. Move to cool area rehydrate and monitor for progression to heat stroke.

question: An athlete collapses during a marathon on a hot day. He is confused and combative. His skin is hot but he is still sweating. Core temperature is estimated at 106°F. Is this heat stroke even though he is sweating?
option_a: No sweating means it cannot be heat stroke
option_b: Yes this is exertional heat stroke. Athletes can still be sweating initially even in heat stroke. Altered mental status with high temperature is the key finding regardless of sweating. Begin immediate aggressive cooling
option_c: He is just exhausted from running
option_d: Wait to see if sweating stops
rationale: Exertional heat stroke in athletes may present with continued sweating unlike classic heat stroke. The key finding is altered mental status with high temperature. Do not wait for dry skin to diagnose heat stroke. Begin cooling immediately.

question: You are cooling a heat stroke patient. What are the most effective cooling methods?
option_a: Give cold water to drink
option_b: Remove clothing apply cool water to skin and fan vigorously. Apply ice packs to neck groin and armpits where large blood vessels are close to surface. If available cold water immersion is most effective
rationale: Effective cooling includes evaporative cooling water plus fanning and conductive cooling ice packs to high blood flow areas. Cold water immersion is most effective when available. Cooling during transport is critical as every minute of elevated temperature causes damage.

question: Hikers find an unresponsive man in the woods during winter. He is cold to touch not shivering and has slow shallow breathing. HR is 40 and weak. He has been missing for 6 hours in 25°F weather. What is his condition?
option_a: Mild hypothermia since he is still alive
option_b: Severe hypothermia. Absence of shivering indicates body cannot generate heat temperature likely below 86°F. Handle extremely gently to avoid triggering arrhythmias. Remove wet clothing insulate and rewarm gradually. Prepare for cardiac arrest
rationale: Absent shivering with unresponsiveness and bradycardia indicates severe hypothermia. The cold heart is extremely irritable. Handle gently remove wet clothing insulate and transport. Be prepared for cardiac arrest and prolonged resuscitation.

question: You are treating a hypothermic patient and she goes into cardiac arrest. You begin CPR and apply the AED which shows a shockable rhythm. After three shocks there is no change. What should you do?
option_a: Stop resuscitation as it is not working
option_b: Continue CPR and transport. Defibrillation may not be effective until the patient is rewarmed. Hypothermia protects organs and patients can survive prolonged arrest if cold. Continue resuscitation until the patient is rewarmed at the hospital
option_c: Only try CPR without more shocks
rationale: Hypothermic hearts may not respond to defibrillation until rewarmed. Continue CPR during transport. Cold protects brain and organs allowing survival after prolonged arrest. The patient is not dead until warm and dead. Hospital rewarming may restore circulation.

question: A homeless man has frostbite on his fingers which are white waxy and hard. He also has hypothermia. He is 30 minutes from the hospital. Should you rewarm his hands?
option_a: Yes rewarm immediately in warm water
option_b: No do not rewarm if there is any chance of refreezing or if transport is available within reasonable time. Protect frozen tissue from further injury and transport. Rewarming in the field risks refreezing which causes more damage than remaining frozen
option_c: Rub his hands to warm them
option_d: Apply ice to prevent further damage
rationale: Frostbitten tissue should not be rewarmed in the field if there is risk of refreezing or if hospital is reachable. Refreezing causes more damage than remaining frozen. Protect tissue from trauma and transport. Hospital can rewarm under controlled conditions.

question: A patient with moderate hypothermia is shivering violently. Why is shivering important and what should you do?
option_a: Shivering is dangerous and should be stopped
option_b: Shivering is the body's heat-generating mechanism and indicates the patient can still produce heat. This is a positive sign. Remove wet clothing provide insulation and passive rewarming. Shivering stops in severe hypothermia when the body cannot generate heat
rationale: Shivering generates heat through muscle activity. Violent shivering indicates moderate hypothermia with intact compensatory mechanisms. This is actually reassuring as severe hypothermia causes shivering to stop. Support with insulation and let the body rewarm.

question: Lifeguards pull an unresponsive 16-year-old from a pool. She was found floating face down and estimated submersion time is 2-3 minutes. She is not breathing and has no pulse. What should you do?
option_a: Start with chest compressions like regular CPR
option_b: Start with rescue breaths. Drowning is primarily a respiratory emergency and hypoxia causes the arrest. Give 5 initial rescue breaths then begin CPR with standard 30:2 ratio. Apply AED when available
option_c: She has been down too long to save
option_d: Wait for ALS before starting CPR
rationale: Drowning causes cardiac arrest through hypoxia so early oxygenation is critical. Current guidelines recommend starting with rescue breaths for drowning victims before compressions if this can be done quickly. Then continue standard CPR with AED.

question: A child is pulled from a cold lake after 15 minutes of submersion. He is pulseless and not breathing. His body is cold. Some bystanders say not to bother with CPR since he has been under too long. What should you do?
option_a: Agree that 15 minutes is too long
option_b: Begin CPR immediately. Cold water drowning has better survival than warm water because hypothermia protects the brain. Children have survived prolonged cold water submersion. Continue resuscitation and transport for hospital rewarming
rationale: Cold water rapidly induces hypothermia which protects the brain. Remarkable survivals have been documented after prolonged cold water submersion especially in children. Begin CPR and continue until hospital rewarming. Do not withhold resuscitation based on submersion time alone.

question: A drowning victim begins vomiting during CPR. Large amounts of water come up. What should you do?
option_a: Continue CPR without stopping
option_b: Turn the patient on their side to allow drainage and suction the airway. Drowning victims often swallow water and vomiting is common during resuscitation. Clear the airway then continue CPR. Be prepared for repeated vomiting
option_c: Perform abdominal thrusts to expel water
option_d: Stop CPR as vomiting means he is alive
rationale: Vomiting is common in drowning resuscitation. Turn to side to allow drainage and suction airway to prevent aspiration. Then continue CPR. Do not use abdominal thrusts which do not expel water from lungs and increase aspiration risk.

question: A teenager was diving into shallow water and is now floating face down. Bystanders pulled him out and he is breathing but cannot move his arms or legs. He has neck pain. What should you suspect and do?
option_a: He is just stunned from the dive
option_b: Suspect cervical spine injury from striking the bottom. Maintain spinal immobilization. Diving into shallow water commonly causes cervical fractures. The paralysis indicates spinal cord involvement. Immobilize carefully and transport emergently
option_c: Help him walk to the ambulance
option_d: Spinal injury is unlikely since he is breathing
rationale: Diving into shallow water is a high-risk mechanism for cervical spine injury. Paralysis of all four extremities indicates cervical cord injury. He is breathing because the injury is below the level controlling the diaphragm. Maintain strict spinal precautions.

question: A 5-year-old is rescued from a pool. After initially appearing fine he is now coughing with increasing respiratory distress 30 minutes later. SpO2 is dropping. What is happening?
option_a: He is having an anxiety attack
option_b: This may be secondary drowning or delayed pulmonary injury. Aspirated water causes inflammation and pulmonary edema that can develop hours after the event. He needs hospital monitoring and treatment for pulmonary complications
option_d: He is fine since he was initially okay
rationale: Secondary drowning occurs when aspirated water causes delayed pulmonary edema and respiratory distress hours after the event. All drowning victims should be monitored even if initially asymptomatic. Worsening symptoms require immediate treatment.

question: A hiker was bitten on the calf by a rattlesnake 20 minutes ago. The bite area is swollen and painful. She is anxious but alert with stable vital signs. What should you do?
option_a: Apply a tourniquet above the bite
option_b: Keep her calm and still. Immobilize the bitten leg at or below heart level. Remove jewelry and tight clothing from the extremity. Mark the edge of swelling with the time. Do not apply tourniquet ice or attempt to suck out venom. Transport for antivenom evaluation
option_c: Have her walk to the ambulance
option_d: Apply ice to slow venom spread
rationale: Snake bite management includes calming the patient immobilizing the extremity at or below heart level removing constrictive items and transporting. Do not apply tourniquet ice or attempt venom extraction which are ineffective and potentially harmful.

question: A man was stung by multiple bees while mowing. He has hives spreading on his chest difficulty breathing and feels his throat swelling. He has an EpiPen. What should you do?
option_a: Give antihistamine and observe
option_b: Help him use his EpiPen immediately. Multiple stings with systemic symptoms including airway involvement indicates anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is life-saving. After EpiPen provide oxygen and transport. He may need additional epinephrine doses
option_c: Wait to see if symptoms worsen
option_d: Hives will resolve on their own
rationale: Multiple bee stings with systemic symptoms and airway involvement is anaphylaxis requiring immediate epinephrine. Assist with EpiPen then provide supportive care and transport. Monitor for symptom recurrence as additional doses may be needed.

question: A patient was bitten by a spider she did not see. The bite occurred 8 hours ago and now has a central blister with surrounding redness and pain. There is no systemic reaction. What spider might this be?
option_a: Black widow
option_b: Possibly brown recluse. Brown recluse bites often are not immediately painful but develop a central blister with tissue destruction over hours to days. The patient should be transported for wound evaluation and monitoring for progression
rationale: Brown recluse bites characteristically develop a central blister or necrotic area hours after the bite. The initial bite may be painless. Medical evaluation is needed to monitor for tissue necrosis and potential systemic effects.

question: A group hiking at 10000 feet has a member with severe headache nausea and vomiting. He is becoming confused and having difficulty walking straight. What condition is developing and what is the treatment?
option_a: Dehydration. Give fluids
option_b: High altitude cerebral edema HACE. Confusion and ataxia at altitude indicate brain swelling which is life-threatening. Immediate descent is critical. Give oxygen if available. Do not continue ascending. He needs to get to lower altitude immediately
rationale: HACE causes brain swelling with confusion ataxia and potentially coma and death. This is an emergency requiring immediate descent. Oxygen helps but descent is definitive treatment. Even descending a few thousand feet can be lifesaving.

question: At high altitude a climber develops severe shortness of breath with cough producing pink frothy sputum. He cannot catch his breath even at rest. SpO2 is 72%. What is happening?
option_a: Asthma attack
option_b: High altitude pulmonary edema HAPE. Fluid accumulation in the lungs at altitude causing severe hypoxia. This is life-threatening. Give high-flow oxygen and descend immediately. HAPE can progress rapidly to death without treatment
rationale: HAPE causes pulmonary edema at altitude with severe dyspnea hypoxia and pink frothy sputum. Immediate descent and oxygen are critical. This can be fatal within hours without treatment. Descent is the definitive treatment.

question: A lightning strike hit a group of people in a park. You arrive to find 5 patients. Two are unconscious and appear dead. Three are sitting up complaining of various symptoms. Who should you treat first?
option_a: The sitting patients since they are obviously alive
option_b: The unconscious patients who appear dead. Lightning strike uses reverse triage. Those who appear dead may have temporary cardiac arrest that responds to CPR. Those who are conscious will likely survive. Prioritize resuscitation of the apparently dead
rationale: Lightning injuries use reverse triage. Lightning causes cardiac arrest through direct current that may respond to immediate CPR. Those who are conscious and breathing will likely survive without immediate intervention. Prioritize the apparently dead.

question: A patient was struck by lightning and is now in cardiac arrest. Is it safe to touch him and what should you do?
option_a: No he may still be electrified
option_b: Yes it is safe. Lightning victims do not retain electrical charge. Immediately begin CPR and apply AED. Lightning commonly causes VF which is shockable. Early CPR and defibrillation can be very successful in lightning strike victims
rationale: Lightning strike victims do not retain electrical charge and are safe to touch immediately. Begin CPR and use AED. Lightning commonly causes VF and survival rates with early CPR and defibrillation are better than for other causes of cardiac arrest.

question: A scuba diver surfaced rapidly from 60 feet due to equipment problems. He now has joint pain confusion and skin mottling 30 minutes after surfacing. What condition is this?
option_a: Nitrogen narcosis
option_b: Decompression sickness the bends. Rapid ascent caused nitrogen bubbles to form in blood and tissues. Symptoms include joint pain neurological problems and skin changes. He needs high-flow oxygen and transport to a facility with hyperbaric chamber
rationale: Decompression sickness occurs when dissolved nitrogen forms bubbles during rapid ascent. Symptoms include joint pain neurological deficits and skin mottling. Treatment requires 100% oxygen and recompression in a hyperbaric chamber.

question: A diver ascending from depth suddenly loses consciousness and has a seizure just below the surface. What emergency is this?
option_a: Decompression sickness
option_b: Arterial gas embolism. Air expanding in the lungs during rapid ascent can enter the bloodstream and travel to the brain causing stroke-like symptoms or seizures. This is immediately life-threatening. Give oxygen and transport emergently for hyperbaric treatment
rationale: Arterial gas embolism occurs when expanding air enters pulmonary vessels and travels to the brain causing immediate neurological symptoms. This is a diving emergency requiring immediate 100% oxygen and hyperbaric treatment. Time is critical.

question: You respond to a reported radiation exposure at a medical facility. A technician was accidentally exposed to a radiation source but is not contaminated with radioactive material. Is it safe to treat this patient?
option_a: No the patient is radioactive
option_b: Yes. Radiation exposure alone does not make a person radioactive. The patient was exposed to radiation but is not contaminated with radioactive material. It is safe to provide normal care without special precautions
rationale: Exposure to radiation like getting an X-ray does not make someone radioactive. The person was exposed but has no radioactive material on or in them. Normal patient care can be provided. Contamination with radioactive material is different and requires decontamination.

question: Workers at a chemical plant report a coworker collapsed after a tank leaked. There is a strong chemical smell. Other workers are fleeing the area. What should you do?
option_a: Rush in to rescue the patient
option_b: Do NOT enter the hazardous area. Stage at a safe distance upwind and call for hazmat team. You cannot help the patient if you become a victim. Wait for properly equipped rescue teams. Begin planning for decontamination and treatment of victims once removed
option_c: Put on surgical mask and enter
option_d: Hold your breath and go in quickly
rationale: Scene safety first. Chemical exposures can incapacitate rescuers within seconds. Do not enter hazmat scenes without proper training and equipment. Stage upwind at a safe distance and request hazmat response. Plan for decontamination and treatment once victims are removed by trained teams.

question: You respond to a home where multiple family members have headaches nausea and dizziness. A gas furnace is running. One person is confused. SpO2 readings are all normal 97-99%. What should you suspect?
option_a: Viral illness affecting the whole family
option_b: Carbon monoxide poisoning. Multiple people with similar symptoms and a combustion source suggest CO. Normal SpO2 is meaningless as pulse oximetry cannot detect CO poisoning. Remove everyone from the home give high-flow oxygen and transport for CO level testing
option_d: Readings are normal so they are fine
rationale: Multiple patients with headache nausea and neurological symptoms with a combustion source suggests CO poisoning. Pulse oximetry cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin so normal SpO2 does not rule out CO poisoning. Remove from exposure and give 100% oxygen.
